file_name: Vázquez Noguera et al. - 2022 - Diagnosis of Melanocytic Lesions using the K-Nearest Neighbors classification technique.json

did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper uses the K-Nearest Neighbors (K-NN) classification algorithm, a machine learning technique, to classify dermoscopic images of melanocytic lesions as benign or malignant. The methodology includes image preprocessing (hair removal, contrast enhancement), segmentation, and feature extraction (asymmetry, border, color, dermoscopic structures) to support the diagnosis of melanoma.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work proposing a methodology for diagnosing melanoma using K-Nearest Neighbors classification. It details specific modules (preprocessing, segmentation, feature extraction, classification), describes experimental setups (two experiments with training/testing splits and K-Fold Cross-Validation), and reports new quantitative results (accuracy, sensitivity, specificity) on a dataset of 120 dermoscopic images. It is published in the proceedings of the 17th Iberian Conference on Information Systems and Technologies (CISTI).

title: Diagnosis of Melanocytic Lesions using the K-Nearest Neighbors classification technique
journal: 2022 17th Iberian Conference on Information Systems and Technologies (CISTI)
brief_summary: This study proposes a methodology to assist dermatologists in the early diagnosis of melanoma using the K-Nearest Neighbors (K-NN) classification algorithm. The approach processes dermoscopic images through four modules: preprocessing for hair removal, segmentation to isolate the lesion, feature extraction of asymmetry, border, color, and dermoscopic structures, and finally classification. The dataset consisted of 120 dermoscopic images, which were evaluated using two experimental setups: a hold-out method and 4-Fold Cross-Validation. The performance was assessed using accuracy, sensitivity, and specificity metrics across various distance measures including Euclidean, Manhattan, Chebyshev, Minkowski, Cosine, and Correlation. The best results were achieved with Euclidean and Minkowski distances in the second experiment, yielding 91.67% accuracy, 92.50% sensitivity, and 100% specificity. The authors conclude that these promising results suggest the technique could serve as a basis for more sophisticated clinical diagnostic tools.

lesion_types_studied.details: Classified as malignant lesions (Yi = 1) in the K-NN classification task. The study focuses on diagnosing melanoma using dermoscopic images.
Classified as benign lesions (Yi = 0) in the K-NN classification task. The dataset contained 120 dermoscopic images of melanocytic lesions, split into benign and malignant classes.

all_task_types.target: Binary diagnostic category (benign vs. malignant melanocytic lesions)
all_task_types.details: The paper uses the K-Nearest Neighbors (K-NN) classifier to predict discrete labels: Yi = 0 for benign lesions and Yi = 1 for malignant lesions (melanoma). The task involves distinguishing between two classes based on extracted features (asymmetry, border, color, dermoscopic structures) from dermoscopic images.

datasets_used.name: Custom Dermoscopic Image Dataset
datasets_used.description: A private dataset of 120 dermoscopic images used for the diagnosis of melanocytic lesions. The paper references a previous work [12] for the dataset details but does not specify the original source or public availability in this text.

use.training: Used in two experimental setups: Experiment I used a hold-out method with 32 images for training (12 benign, 20 malignant) and 72 for validation; Experiment II used 4-Fold Cross-Validation where data was split into training and testing groups per fold.
use.validation: Used in Experiment I (hold-out method) with 72 images designated for validation of the classifier.
use.testing: Used in Experiment I (hold-out method) with 48 images for testing (implied by 120 total - 32 training - 72 validation, though Table I shows 56 malignant and 16 benign in test set for Exp I which sums to 72, indicating the 72 were split between test and validation or the text description of '72 images used for validation' might refer to the test set in the hold-out context, but Table I clarifies the split: 32 training, 72 test. The text says '72 images... used for validation' but Table I labels them 'Prueba' (Test). We will list testing as the primary role for the non-training set).

statistical_methods_used.purpose: To evaluate the performance of the K-NN classifier by dividing the dataset into K groups and performing K iterations, using one group for testing and the others for training in each iteration.
To split the dataset into complementary sets for training and testing/validation in the first experiment.
To calculate the average performance metrics (sensitivity, specificity, accuracy) across the different folds or iterations of the experiments.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance was evaluated using descriptive statistics (accuracy, sensitivity, specificity) and arithmetic means across experimental folds.

Experiment I (Hold-out Method).description: Training set: 32 images (12 benign, 20 malignant). Test set: 72 images (16 benign, 56 malignant). Varied K (1, 5, 10) and distance metrics.
Experiment I (Hold-out Method).notes: The paper notes that for Euclidean and Minkowski distances, increasing K led to significant increases in specificity and accuracy.

Experiment II (4-Fold Cross-Validation).description: Data split into 4 groups. Mean arithmetic results reported across folds.
Experiment II (4-Fold Cross-Validation).notes: The paper highlights that the best results were obtained with Euclidean and Minkowski distances, achieving 91.67% sensitivity, 100% specificity, and 90% accuracy (mean). The abstract reports 91.67% accuracy, 92.50% sensitivity, and 100% specificity, which appears to correspond to specific fold results or a slight discrepancy in the abstract vs table V means, but Table V is the detailed experimental result.

feature_vector.description: Feature vector x = {Ca, Cb, Cc, Ced} extracted from segmented dermoscopic images.

training_details.training_method: Two-stage process: 1. Build classifier with training set. 2. Evaluate with test set.
training_details.hold_out_split: 32 images for training (12 benign, 20 malignant), 72 images for testing (16 benign, 56 malignant)

preprocessing_techniques: Hair removal using Bottom-hat operator, Otsu binarization, and Inpainting algorithm
Contrast enhancement using CLAHE (Contrast Limited Adaptive Histogram Equalization)
Automatic Maximum Entropy Thresholding for binary mask generation
Area filtering (removing areas smaller than 30 pixels)
conclusions: The proposed methodology using K-Nearest Neighbors (K-NN) classification, supported by preprocessing, segmentation, and feature extraction (asymmetry, border, color, dermoscopic structures), shows promising results for assisting dermatologists in the diagnosis of melanoma.
The best performance was achieved using Euclidean and Minkowski distance metrics, yielding 91.67% accuracy, 92.50% sensitivity, and 100% specificity.
The technique has potential to serve as a basis for more sophisticated clinical diagnostic tools for melanoma.
The study acknowledges that while many works address this problem, there are currently no globally recognized diagnostic tools for dermatologists, highlighting the need for such assistance.
important_remarks: The study uses a very small, private dataset of only 120 dermoscopic images, limiting statistical power and generalizability.
The dataset source is not publicly available; it references a previous work [12] for details, preventing independent replication.
There is a significant class imbalance in the test set of Experiment I (56 malignant vs. 16 benign), which may bias performance metrics.
The paper does not report external validation on independent datasets, raising concerns about overfitting to the specific collection.
No formal statistical hypothesis testing (e.g., p-values, confidence intervals) was performed; results are reported as descriptive statistics.
The methodology relies on manual feature extraction (asymmetry, border, color, structures) rather than end-to-end deep learning, which may limit scalability and robustness compared to modern CNNs.

The abstract reports slightly different metrics (91.67% accuracy, 92.50% sensitivity) than the detailed Table V for Euclidean distance (90.00% accuracy, 91.67% sensitivity), indicating potential reporting inconsistencies.
The paper acknowledges that no globally recognized diagnostic tools currently exist, highlighting the exploratory nature of this work rather than a validated clinical tool.
